# Extended Case References — Lecture PM6Qc720MgA

*Brief mentions of canonical cases that did not warrant a full `case_index.md` entry. Generated by reconciliation pass against the 1,676-cluster canon. Substantive treatments are in `case_index.md`.*

## Cases mentioned in passing

### Regional jet wing flap heating system design case
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Regional jet wing flap heating system design case"
- **Anchor:** `§2.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "For the aircraft example, I want to talk about the front flap of an aircraft wing. I worked in small aircraft — they were building the original regional jets, so when you take a shuttle from here to DC or from here to Cleveland, the shuttle type aircraft for 70 to 90 passengers will have this design where the engine is not right here, it's actually close to the tail in the back, and that makes the wings a little smaller. So it's a very narrow and elongated design. Even when your wings are smaller, you're more dependent on them. You don't want them covered with ice when you're going through a..."

### Aircraft wing casting substitution project
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Aircraft wing casting substitution project"
- **Anchor:** `§4.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "When you look at it, that's an example of substitution. Honestly, they didn't really know all the loads that could get onto that component. They were looking in a stress analysis at different scenarios, combining them, but there was a little bit of a match with what happened in flight tests. They had gauges that would look at the stresses at locations they knew were going to be high-stress locations from the design standpoint, from the computer analysis. They still had to calibrate all that with an actual physical test. The ultimate test before you could fly that wing was attaching it to a..."

### Landing gear torsional cracking failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Landing gear torsional cracking failure"
- **Anchor:** `§4.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "There is another project I was working on at the same time, looking at the landing gear, where in those tests where they were just applying about twice the load that it would see at service, they had this crack growing really fast. Because of a torsional effect on one of the structural components that they never really analyzed for torsion — they did the tension, the bending, but they never really thought torsion would become a dominant mode. So it was a good way to identify the problem — not so early, because they already had the weight of the aircraft sold for. And it was a very big heavy..."

### Tomato greenhouse/warehouse weld failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Tomato greenhouse/warehouse weld failure"
- **Anchor:** `§8.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "That's a big warehouse for tomatoes — greenhouse — that I worked on. Same story, where you look at this weld and we talked a little bit about convexity. So maybe today you'd be able to say, if you go somewhere and you see this, oh, this looks like it's a little sort of deposited on the surface, as opposed to being really well fused. It has a low contact angle here. This is the same type of weld that, after the fact, you can see it just pull off from the surface of that chord, as opposed to ripping the metal apart with it."

### Propeller shaft fatigue failure (boat/sport unit)
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Propeller shaft fatigue failure (boat/sport unit)"
- **Anchor:** `§9.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "The probability of failure — we touched on that again at the beginning — there will be a different safety factor, or a final acceptable safety factor, for different types of structures. So I'm not going to rule that out. We can't say, we're over-designing everything by 20 percent. It's not true. It will vary depending on how willing you are to risk a failure. We had that propeller shaft example that was designed to fail within 400 hours, and therefore the safety factor for a 10-year life is less than one. It's just the way it is."

### Shaft designed to fail within 400 hours
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Shaft designed to fail within 400 hours"
- **Anchor:** `§9.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "The probability of failure — we touched on that again at the beginning — there will be a different safety factor, or a final acceptable safety factor, for different types of structures. So I'm not going to rule that out. We can't say, we're over-designing everything by 20 percent. It's not true. It will vary depending on how willing you are to risk a failure. We had that propeller shaft example that was designed to fail within 400 hours, and therefore the safety factor for a 10-year life is less than one. It's just the way it is."
